name: Accessible Name Intent
summary: Cross-cutting policy for an element's accessible name — whether it carries meaning at all, and where its label comes from. Owns the declarative naming decision only; name computation is deferred to the platform W3C AccName 1.2 algorithm (not re-specified, not a protocol). Two axes: naming (meaningful | decorative) and labelSource (explicit | referenced | derived-fallback). Most-permissive default: meaningful + derived-fallback, where the fallback is never empty (CLDR short name for emoji, alt for images); custom imagery with no inherent text requires an explicit label. icon/glyph/media/image/avatar compose this rather than each redefining naming.

naming.description: Whether the element contributes an accessible name at all, or is purely decorative

labelSource.description: Where the accessible name comes from when the element is meaningful

description:
Naming

Whether the element carries meaning for assistive technology, or is purely presentational.

  • meaningful (default): the element contributes an accessible name. The most-permissive default — naming is assumed unless the author opts out.
  • decorative: the element is presentational and is removed from the accessibility tree (aria-hidden="true" / empty alt=""). An explicit author opt-in, never inferred.

Label Source

Where a meaningful element's name comes from. This intent decides the source; it does not restate how the browser resolves the name.

  • explicit: an author-supplied string (e.g. aria-label or a visible text label).
  • referenced: the name is taken from other element(s) by id via aria-labelledby.
  • derived-fallback (default): no author label — the name is derived from the element's own content, and the fallback is never empty: the CLDR short name for an emoji (per #587), the alt text for an image. A custom image/sticker with no inherent text has no fallback ⇒ a label is required, not defaulted.

Most-permissive default

meaningful + derived-fallback: an element is named, from its own content, with no author effort — and degrades to a CLDR/alt fallback rather than an empty name. The restrictive cases (decorative, an explicit required label) are author opt-ins.

Computation is the platform's job

This intent deliberately does not re-specify the AccName precedence (aria-labelledby > aria-label > native > title) — that is the browser's W3C Accessible Name and Description Computation 1.2 algorithm (native-first, minimize-lock-in). It is a UX naming policy, not a technical interchange contract: no adapters.json entry, no Technical Configurator card.

Composition

Symbol- and media-bearing intents compose this instead of each redefining naming: icon/glyph/media/image/avatar (#587). #587's interim accessible-name dimension graduates to composing this intent now that it is minted.

designSystemResearch.keyInsight: The accessible-name computation (aria-labelledby > aria-label > native > title) is a standardized platform algorithm. A standard should declare the naming policy and defer computation to it rather than re-implementing precedence.

Decorative elements should be hidden from the accessibility tree (aria-hidden / empty alt); meaningful elements must always resolve to a non-empty name. The decorative-vs-meaningful split is the primary authoring decision.
Emoji carry a localized CLDR short name (e.g. '😀' → 'grinning face') usable as a never-empty derived fallback, so a bare emoji is never announced as an unnamed glyph.
